sentences.text: My name is Gerda Haas.
H-A-A-S.
My first name is spelled G-E-R-D-A.
My name was Gerda Buchheim.
B-U-C-H-H-E-I-M.
My birthday is April 11, and I was born in 1914.
In Berlin, Germany.
My age at this taping is 82.
Sounds terrible.
[LAUGHS]
It was just fine.
It was like people live here as Jewish people.
There we were accepted by everybody.
We had a kosher butcher shop, so we had to do, only, really, business-- no, I mean in the store, with Jewish people, we had kosher meat.
But we mingled with just everybody else, and in the school, there wasn't any difference.
And the public schools had religion there, so we went to the Jewish religion as ours, and Christians or-- went to theirs.
There wasn't any problem.
We lived in an apartment house.
We knew everybody.
There were three, four, five Jewish families, maybe 50 non-Jewish ones.
When they had Christmas, we helped them celebrate.
They came to our Hanukkah, and it was fine.
My father was a six foot tall man who worked around the clock, and all my parents wanted, both of them, that we children make something more out of-- more schooling than what they had.

My father, especially, [BACKROUND NOISE] had to quit school when he was 12 years old and learned the butcher trade, because there were too many children in the family, and the house-- they didn't re-model their house like they do here and build on.
The oldest, or the older people, have to go.
Meyer Buchheim, but in Berlin, he was called Max.
But his real name is Meyer.
M-E-Y-E-R.
He was born April 7, 1884.
In a little town called Rosenthal.
It was in Hessen.
That was like a province, not too far from Berlin, but it wasn't in Berlin.
In Germany, yes.
Rosenthal?
R-O-S-E-N-T-H-A-L.
My mother's name was Paula Rosenthal, before she got married, and they were always joking about it.
The judge says, where were you born, he said in Rosenthal.
He said, I know your wife's maiden name is Rosenthal.
Where were you born?
He said, in Rosenthal.
[LAUGHS]
Rosenthal?
R-O-S-E-N-T-H-A-L.
Yeah Yeah, I had one sister.
She was five years older.
She passed away three years ago in San Francisco.
Her name was Hilde Benjamin.
Just nothing but fun, and I was a good-- good student.
I didn't have any problems.
I volunteered here and there.
I volunteered to go out and buy a Christmas tree for-- for a party, and my father met me.
He said, there's nobody else to do it.
I said, they don't want to do it, and I like it.
So it was just nothing but fun.
We had a good time.
We had a rowing club, and I was rowing.
I was swimming.
I was bicycling.
I-- no worry, whatsoever.
And how did the teachers treat you?
Fine.
There wasn't any difference till 1933.
We were kind of Orthodox.
We kept all the traditions and went to the synagogue and kept all the holidays.
We're a traditional family.
Yeah.
We went to the temple, and after that was a kind of a bigger meal at lunch time.
And it was very common, like, my parents took their nap on Saturday afternoon.
We couldn't disturb them.
That was the only day in the week where they had a little more rest.
And we kids, we went for a walk and-- with the neighbor kids, or with other Jewish kids who had-- couldn't ride the street car or bus or ride a bicycle.
So we met in the park or we went for a walk, and then came back home when Shabbos was out and we made half dollar, and then normal times were back again.
We could do everything, what we don't-- didn't do on Shabbos.
We didn't write on Shabbos, for instance.
We didn't carry stuff.
So we were kind of glad when it was over.
We didn't like the restrictions too well.
Yes.
Yes, it was-- there were lots of Jews in the neighborhood.
Synagogues were there, and another kosher butcher, and my-- the neighbors there became my in-laws later on.
They had a grocery store in the neighborhood, and their apartment was in the same apartment building.
So I grew up with the Haas family.
I was just one of them when I was already 12 years old, 13 years old.
And what was the name of the synagogue you attended?
We went-- the name was [NON-ENGLISH].
That was the street where this temple was on.
But we just went to [NON-ENGLISH].
That was the name.
It was a conservative one.
Yes.
I don't remember how many, but lots of synagogues were large congregations, because there were a lot, a lot of people-- Jewish people in Berlin.
Well, everything was interrupted.
Like I remember that one boy in the same apartment house, he spat at us when we walked up and down the stairs and met him.
And I looked out of a window in the hallway to the left, and there was my school, and they were burning books.
And--
Must have been around '33.
And we couldn't-- of course it went a little-- gradually.
We couldn't go into parks anymore.
We couldn't sit on the bench in a public park, couldn't go to theaters.
We couldn't go to the movies.
So to begin with, every-- especially the older people, like my parents, they thought it will go by.
So we formed our own cultural events, as operas and-- not in big opera houses or what, but we did our own cultural things, because we were used to going to the theater, movies, and operas.
Do you recall your first-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] the first time you noticed the Nazi power affecting your life?
Well, that came, actually, overnight.
Rapidly, rapidly.
I had a private Hebrew teacher with me started talking conversational Hebrew.
I remember the story I read.
And I looked out the window, and we heard these terrible songs and saw them marching.
And I said, oh, what is that now?
It's really--
The Nazis in the brown uniforms, and the SS, which was worse, was in the black uniforms with the black boots.
It was also-- everything, '33 and '34.
Hilde.
Well, my sister got married in '33, and my father bought my brother-in-law and her a store, a Jewish were-- owners-- the owners were Jewish and left Germany.
And I said to my father, why do you buy them a store?
These Jewish people, they left, and what do you think Hilde and her husband will have to do?
Oh, don't talk that way.
He will go by.
We had other bad people.
They go down, and-- and we will stay.
So they had a good business, but it didn't make any sense.
These people had a reason to leave, the Jewish people who owned the store.
But they didn't want to let us go, and we weren't too happy either, to leave.
It was a very strange idea that we get away from where we were born and raised.
Yeah.
We had the store in the front, and in the back was an office.
And our daily meals we took and ate there too.
And then there were the rooms where the people worked.
My father made sausage a lot, and he canned goods, like he made chicken soup and beef soup and-- for sale, wholesale and retail.
And then there was a kitchen where we cooked.
We didn't cook.
We had hired people.
We-- everybody worked in the store.
I had to deliver packages when I was little, and I could take cash when I was six years old.
It was a business household.
And upstairs, there were our living quarters-- sleeping quarters.
It was a nice apartment, but all we did was go up there, took showers or baths, and came back and-- actual living was downstairs behind the store.
Even the holidays, like the Seder, with 20 people or what, was downstairs.
We never used that upstairs.
With my parents.
Mother, father, and we had uncle and aunt partners in the business.
They had no children.
They lived with-- they didn't live with us.
We ate with them.
They had their own apartment somewhere else.
Schey.
S-C-H-E-Y.
She was my mother's sister.
Adolf, sorry to say was his name, and I don't even like to say that name.
And Cilla, C-I-L-L-A.
They both got killed too.
Everybody.
All the sisters and brothers of my parents, except one or two, where he came to America.
No.
The girls that helped us, they-- they stayed with us, yes.
Upstairs in the apartment, there were rooms for them.
[LAUGHS] One was Emilia, which would be Emilie.
But the others sometimes changed pretty often.
I don't know.
But Emilia actually raised me.
I confided in her, and she talked more to me than mother.
And she said, I don't know what the Nazis want.
You've been good to us-- to me-- for over 20 years, and I can't find any fault with Jewish people.
But look, they're sending me in vacation now.
I've never been in vacation.
They're sending me to Italy.
Isn't that nice?
And I said, yeah, it's nice, but that's how they want you to join the party.
And pretty soon after that, she wasn't allowed to work for us anymore.
She wasn't Jewish.
No.
No.
No.
My -- my parents didn't want to know about that.
They-- family, my uncles and aunts from other town, they called my father, and they were butchers too.
And they said, it was a time where he could have packed up all the machinery he had.
And my parents had the money to go to, at that time, Palestine.
And they would have been allowed in as business people, so to say, with the machinery and saying we want to open up a butcher store.
They could have gotten into the country.
But my uncles and aunts went.
And my parents said, we go to-- if we go anywhere, we're going to America.
We have a nephew there and a sister-in-law and brother-in-law.
They will help us.
But they didn't start the process.
Not early enough.
Too late.
I guess like anybody else.
Have your husband and a child, maybe two.
And that's all.
And have a good husband, has to make the good living, so we can travel.
I always liked to travel.
And we did, as children.
Once a year, mother took us.
Father almost never had time, but sometimes he had to back down and go with us.
Some resort places and spas.
My mother had, I think it was called gout, and, you know, for every sickness they had a different spa.
This aunt had stomach trouble.
She went somewhere else.
And people believed, though, the doctors at that time believed it was the springs and taking baths in the spring water.
And that's what we did.
Of course, we children didn't.
We just liked to roam around.
Yeah.
I wanted to be a teacher or a social worker.
That was absolutely-- I knew that since I was 12 years old, only I couldn't make up my mind which one.
And then, when I did go through the school that I was eligible to go to the university, they asked me for my religion, and of course I said Jewish.
And they said, get out of here.
Jews are not allowed.
Berlin.
University in Berlin.
And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] my father said, I was in the first World War, and I won an Iron Cross for bravery.
And I'm going to take my Iron Cross and go with you.
They're not going to do that to you.
So he went with me, and they saw him out too.
So he didn't want to believe it.
Everything that's happening, what happened, they-- they didn't-- just couldn't believe it.
What was your father in the First World War?
I don't know.
Just, probably-- I don't know even the ranks here.
Just a foot soldier, nothing special.
Yeah, in-- in-- in the east, in Romania, Russia.
That's what I always heard.
And he took me-- he never liked the movies or what, but he took me to war pictures.
It's when pictures of the First World War were on, and he said, let's go.
That's the only thing he wanted to see.
Well, there was a state school where I could get a certificate as a kindergarten teacher.
So I went for one year for that training.
And of course, my parents didn't like that either.
They said I should-- if we think about leaving, we should learn a trade.
I wasn't handy, and I didn't know what to do.
I-- it's all I wanted, to have children around me and have to do-- teach children.
No.
No.
I had my report card here somewhere, but it's one thing I can't find.
I graduated after one year, and I got a certificate.
After that, I could only work for Jewish people.
So I worked in-- went into the camps, like in the summertime, and in day care, day camps.
Jewish.
I couldn't work anywhere else.
Well, it must have been '35, '36, something like that.
Yeah, no, I got married in '35 already, so that all must have been '33, '34.
Of course, when I got married, I didn't work anymore.
I worked-- helped my husband in our own business.
He was in the fur business, wholesale.
And we had an apartment and maybe four rooms.
And part of it was living quarters, and the rest was a business.
But it was wholesale.
So he traveled, and he went out selling.
And he also went to a place-- it's a-- to a city, it's called Leipzig, where the wholesale market was.
And then he was there, he called me to pick him up from the train station.
So I went to the train station and walked up the stairs, and somebody with a big nose and big limp, and short man, walked down the steps, and I recognized him immediately.
It was Goebbels.
And everybody raised their hand and said, 'Heil, Hitler,' and I did not.
So he spoke to me, and he said, how come you don't raise your hand?
I said, because I'm Jewish.
At that time, you could still do that.
What year was that?
It was also-- we were just married. '35, it must have been in '35.
No child yet.
So could have been '36.
We didn't have a child til '38.
And--
Well, their pictures were all over, so you couldn't miss him.
Especially him, you couldn't miss at all.
We also went once to-- behind our parents' back, before I was married, I went with my then boyfriend to a place where Hitler spoke.
And we heard him scream.
I guess you all heard some speeches from him at some time, screaming, yelling, and they all sang the national anthem and raised their hand.
And we didn't either.
At-- we just enjoyed ourselves, you know, out of spite.
And at that time, you could afford to do that.
Later on, you couldn't.
You would have been taken to a camp immediately.
In Berlin.
It was also in Berlin.
Yeah.
In Berlin, it was Hans.
It was his nickname.
He was born Ivan, Ivan, so here in America he called himself Ivan H.
Haas.
And we took the Z off here in America.
Well, he was looking the whole time, and his people he worked with, they're a lot of furriers and fur trade-- in the fur trade.
He wasn't a furrier, he-- but he knew a little bit about it.
He could have worked as a furrier.
He-- they went to Australia.
So he came home one day, and he said, we can go to Australia if we leave now, immediately.
And I-- When was this?
Huh?
I was pregnant, so-- the boy was born in April, '38.
So it must have been in end of '37.
And I said, I'm not going before that baby is born.
I wanted my parents around if a boy-- you know, you have a bris milah, and I couldn't think of that without my father who had two daughters and wanted a boy so badly.
So I said, I'm not leaving, and I want to have this baby right here, where your parents are, where my parents are.
And that's what we're going to do.
We stay till he's born, and then I promise, whatever we're going to do, we go.
When was your son born?
In Berlin.
His name is Henry.
We gave him this name right away because we were-- knew we were going to leave Germany and we didn't want to give him a German name.
And the only reason we could do that was that we were Czechoslovakian citizens, even we were both born in Berlin.
My father-in-law was Hungarian.
It's a long story.
Then this-- the place where he was born became Czechoslovakian, so he had a Czechoslovakian passport, and the kids were all Czechoslovakian citizens.
And the boys didn't really want to go into the Czech army, but they had to go say, we're ready to go.
But they weren't taken because they couldn't speak a word of Czech and they'd even never been there.
So when I married my husband, I lost my German citizenship and became a Czechoslovakian citizen.
And my father said, that's a good thing about that marriage.
[LAUGHTER] But, I mean, that was a prime reason, he said, that was the best, that I lost my German citizenship.
So we were free to travel.
And where did you travel to?
Well, we just-- Australia wasn't-- we couldn't go anymore.
Australia wasn't available anymore.
So I pretended I go on a trip and took the baby.
He was three months old and went to Czechoslovakia, where my father-in-law was born and had family there to help me.
So we stayed there-- the father-in-law had to leave Germany.
They claimed he had-- for tax evasion, which of course wasn't true.
There-- he was there since he was 17 years old.
That was 1884, and '94-- 1901.
And this was 1938.
So anyways, they found faults with his tax return, and he had to leave Germany.
And he joined me in Nitra, is the town.
How do you spell that?
N-I-T-R-A.
And after staying there for a while, then my husband came and joined us.
He left-- left a little later.
He had to sell what little bit we had-- some furniture.
But you-- and the business.
But you didn't get any money for anything.
These people knew we had to leave.
So whatever they gave us, we had to take.
And he came and joined me.
And then in Nitra, one of the-- one day the Hungarians rode into town, on horses.
And the first thing they did, they smashed the windows of the Jewish people, the stores, and it was a Shabbat where everybody was closed.
So my father-in-law was very [BACKROUND NOISE] Hungarian feeling, and he said, now that's it.
That's over with now.
No, I really don't.
He was born in April, and he was a year old, then, when we were in Prague, the following year.
So during this year, we went from [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Nitra.
We made our way to Prague.
Everything before he was a year old.
That year was from '38 to '39, where we were running from one country to another.

Well, we walked to the next train station with a hand cart with a baby buggy on it and everything what he needed, and-- just like the Fiddler on the Roof in the film, That's how we left Nitra and walked to the next train station.
Went on.
We didn't care where the train went, just went on and happened to go to Prague.
So we got off, went to Prague, went to a hotel where a lot of other Jewish refugees were living.
So we stayed there for a while, and tried to get out of Europe.
Who did you stay with in Prague?
In the hotel.
Oh my father-in-law, my husband, and the baby.
We had one room in the hotel, and my husband ran around all day long with the rest of the people.
What do we know?
What are we going to do now?
The only thing is we were there legally, and so we had a little time.
So we tried-- one day he came home, and he said, we can go to Palestine.
And I said, you have to come into the office and listen to what they have to say.
So they told us if we go to Palestine, we have to go on a ship where they cover us up when other ships come around so they don't see that they had people on the ship.
And when we get off, there's a rope ladder we have to climb down, and the-- Is-- not Israel, Palestinian Jewish underground will be there and help us on little boats go to shore.
And then I said, and the British people will shoot at us.
And they said, yeah, that's possible.
I really don't-- probably the underground, the Palestinian underground.
And then he went to another office, the Santa Domingo consulate.
They sold passports there.
And it turned out he hadn't paid yet.
He wanted to buy for everybody else-- my parents and his mother, who was still in Germany.
And it turned out before he paid that the people who bought these visas, they couldn't get on any ship.
The consul took it on themself to collect the money, but there was no transportation.
